metadata.title: AWS Security Hub Controls
metadata.remarks: EXPERIMENTAL USE ONLY. This Open Security Controls Assessment Language (OSCAL) content has been prepared by AWS for informational purposes only. This OSCAL package is experimental and may contain errors or inconsistencies with the authoritative version of published information. Future versions of this OSCAL content, if any, may not be backwards compatible. Our goals for this experiment are to understand customer interest and use-cases for machine-readable compliance artifacts. To that end, AWS needs your [feedback](https://github.com/awslabs/oscal-content-for-aws-services/issues/new) about the quality and usefulness of this content.

parts.name: overview
parts.prose: This control checks whether an AWS Certificate Manager (ACM) certificate is renewed within the specified time period. It checks both imported certificates and certificates provided by ACM. The control fails if the certificate isn't renewed within the specified time period. Unless you provide a custom parameter value for the renewal period, Security Hub uses a default value of 30 days.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Imported and ACM-issued certificates should be renewed after a specified time period

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: For information on how to correct this issue, consult the AWS Security Hub controls documentation.

parts.name: overview
parts.prose: This control checks whether RSA certificates managed by AWS Certificate Manager use a key length of at least 2,048 bits. The control fails if the key length is smaller than 2,048 bits.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: RSA certificates managed by ACM should use a key length of at least 2,048 bits

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: For information on how to correct this issue, consult the AWS Security Hub controls documentation.

parts.name: overview
parts.prose: This control checks whether all stages of an Amazon API Gateway REST or WebSocket API have logging enabled. The control fails if the 'loggingLevel' isn't 'ERROR' or 'INFO' for all stages of the API. Unless you provide custom parameter values to indicate that a specific log type should be enabled, Security Hub produces a passed finding if the logging level is either 'ERROR' or 'INFO'.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: API Gateway REST and WebSocket API execution logging should be enabled

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: For information on how to correct this issue, consult the AWS Security Hub controls documentation.

parts.name: overview
parts.prose: This control checks whether an API Gateway V2 integration has HTTPS enabled for private connections. The control fails if a private connection doesn't have TLS configured.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: API Gateway V2 integrations should use HTTPS for private connections

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: For information on how to correct this issue, consult the AWS Security Hub controls documentation.

parts.name: overview
parts.prose: This control checks whether Amazon API Gateway REST API stages have SSL certificates configured that backend systems can use to authenticate that incoming requests are from the API Gateway.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: API Gateway REST API stages should be configured to use SSL certificates for backend authentication

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: For information on how to correct this issue, consult the AWS Security Hub controls documentation.

parts.name: overview
parts.prose: This control checks whether AWS X-Ray active tracing is enabled for your Amazon API Gateway REST API stages.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: API Gateway REST API stages should have AWS X-Ray tracing enabled

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: For information on how to correct this issue, consult the AWS Security Hub controls documentation.

parts.name: overview
parts.prose: This control checks to see if an API Gateway stage is using an AWS WAF Web ACL. This control fails if an AWS WAF Web ACL is not attached to a REST API Gateway stage.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: API Gateway should be associated with a WAF Web ACL

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: For information on how to correct this issue, consult the AWS Security Hub controls documentation.

parts.name: overview
parts.prose: This control checks if Amazon API Gateway routes have an authorization type. The control fails if the API Gateway route doesn't have any authorization type. Optionally, you can provide a custom parameter value if you want the control to pass only if the route uses the authorization type specified in the 'authorizationType' parameter.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: API Gateway routes should specify an authorization type

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: For information on how to correct this issue, consult the AWS Security Hub controls documentation.

parts.name: overview
parts.prose: This control checks if Amazon API Gateway V2 stages have access logging configured. This control fails if access log settings aren’t defined.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Access logging should be configured for API Gateway V2 Stages

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: For information on how to correct this issue, consult the AWS Security Hub controls documentation.

parts.name: overview
parts.prose: This control checks if an Amazon Web Services (AWS) account has security contact information. The control fails if security contact information is not provided for the account.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Security contact information should be provided for an AWS account.

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: For information on how to correct this issue, consult the AWS Security Hub controls documentation.

parts.name: overview
parts.prose: This control checks if an AWS account is part of an AWS Organizations organization. The control fails if the account is not part of an organization.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: AWS account should be part of an AWS Organizations organization

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: For information on how to correct this issue, consult the AWS Security Hub controls documentation.

parts.name: overview
parts.prose: This control checks whether an AWS Amplify app has the tag keys specified by the requiredKeyTags parameter. The control fails if the app doesn't have any tag keys, or it doesn't have all the keys specified by the requiredKeyTags parameter. If you don't specify any values for the requiredKeyTags parameter, the control checks only for the existence of a tag key and fails if the app doesn't have any tag keys. The control ignores system tags, which are applied automatically and have the aws: prefix.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Amplify apps should be tagged

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: For information on how to correct this issue, consult the AWS Security Hub controls documentation.

parts.name: overview
parts.prose: This control checks whether an AWS Amplify branch has the tag keys specified by the requiredKeyTags parameter. The control fails if the branch doesn't have any tag keys, or it doesn't have all the keys specified by the requiredKeyTags parameter. If you don't specify any values for the requiredKeyTags parameter, the control checks only for the existence of a tag key and fails if the branch doesn't have any tag keys. The control ignores system tags, which are applied automatically and have the aws: prefix.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Amplify branches should be tagged

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: For information on how to correct this issue, consult the AWS Security Hub controls documentation.

parts.name: overview
parts.prose: This control checks whether an AWS AppConfig application has tags with the specific keys defined in the parameter requiredKeyTags. The control fails if the application doesn't have any tag keys or if it doesn't have all the keys specified in the parameter requiredKeyTags. If the parameter requiredKeyTags isn't provided, the control only checks for the existence of a tag key and fails if the application isn't tagged with any key. System tags, which are automatically applied and begin with aws:, are ignored.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: AWS AppConfig applications should be tagged

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: For information on how to correct this issue, consult the AWS Security Hub controls documentation.

parts.name: overview
parts.prose: This control checks whether an AWS AppConfig configuration profile has tags with the specific keys defined in the parameter requiredKeyTags. The control fails if the configuration profile doesn't have any tag keys or if it doesn't have all the keys specified in the parameter requiredKeyTags. If the parameter requiredKeyTags isn't provided, the control only checks for the existence of a tag key and fails if the configuration profile isn't tagged with any key. System tags, which are automatically applied and begin with aws:, are ignored.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: AWS AppConfig configuration profiles should be tagged

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: For information on how to correct this issue, consult the AWS Security Hub controls documentation.

parts.name: statement

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: For information on how to correct this issue, consult the AWS Security Hub controls documentation.

System tags, which are automatically applied and begin with aws:, are ignored.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: AWS AppConfig extension associations should be tagged

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: For information on how to correct this issue, consult the AWS Security Hub controls documentation.

parts.name: guidance

The control fails if the App Runner service doesn't have any tag keys or if it doesn't have all the keys specified in the parameter requiredKeyTags. If the parameter requiredKeyTags isn't provided, the control only checks for the existence of a tag key and fails if the App Runner service isn't tagged with any key. System tags, which are automatically applied and begin with aws:, are ignored.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: App Runner services should be tagged

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: For information on how to correct this issue, consult the AWS Security Hub controls documentation.

parts.name: overview
parts.prose: This control checks whether an AWS App Runner VPC connector has tags with the specific keys defined in the parameter requiredKeyTags. The control fails if the VPC connector doesn't have any tag keys or if it doesn't have all the keys specified in the parameter requiredKeyTags. If the parameter requiredKeyTags isn't provided, the control only checks for the existence of a tag key and fails if the VPC connector isn't tagged with any key. System tags, which are automatically applied and begin with aws:, are ignored.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: App Runner VPC connectors should be tagged

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: For information on how to correct this issue, consult the AWS Security Hub controls documentation.

parts.name: overview
parts.prose: This control checks whether an AWS AppSync API cache is encrypted at rest. The control fails if the API cache isn't encrypted at rest.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: AWS AppSync API caches should be encrypted at rest

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: For information on how to correct this issue, consult the AWS Security Hub controls documentation.

parts.name: overview
parts.prose: This control checks whether an AWS AppSync API has field-level logging turned on. The control fails if the field resolver log level is set to 'None'. Unless you provide custom parameter values to indicate that a specific log type should be enabled, Security Hub produces a passed finding if the field resolver log level is either 'ERROR' or 'ALL'.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: AWS AppSync should have field-level logging enabled

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: For information on how to correct this issue, consult the AWS Security Hub controls documentation.

parts.name: overview
parts.prose: This control checks whether your application uses an API key to interact with an AWS AppSync GraphQL API. The control fails if an AWS AppSync GraphQL API is authenticated with an API key.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: AWS AppSync GraphQL APIs should not be authenticated with API keys

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: For information on how to correct this issue, consult the AWS Security Hub controls documentation.

parts.name: overview
parts.prose: This control checks whether an AWS AppSync API cache is encrypted in transit. The control fails if the API cache isn't encrypted in transit.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: AWS AppSync API caches should be encrypted in transit

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: For information on how to correct this issue, consult the AWS Security Hub controls documentation.

parts.name: overview
parts.prose: This control checks whether an Amazon Athena workgroup has logging enabled. The control fails if the workgroup doesn't have logging enabled.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Athena workgroups should have logging enabled

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: For information on how to correct this issue, consult the AWS Security Hub controls documentation.

parts.name: overview
parts.prose: This control checks whether an Amazon EC2 Auto Scaling group that is associated with a load balancer uses Elastic Load Balancing (ELB) health checks. The control fails if the Auto Scaling group doesn't use ELB health checks.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Auto scaling groups associated with a load balancer should use ELB health checks

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: For information on how to correct this issue, consult the AWS Security Hub controls documentation.

parts.name: overview
parts.prose: This control checks whether an Amazon EC2 Auto Scaling group spans at least the specified number of Availability Zones (AZs). The control fails if an Auto Scaling group doesn't span at least the specified number of AZs. Unless you provide a custom parameter value for the minimum number of AZs, Security Hub uses a default value of two AZs.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Amazon EC2 Auto Scaling group should cover multiple Availability Zones

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: For information on how to correct this issue, consult the AWS Security Hub controls documentation.

parts.name: overview
parts.prose: This control checks whether IMDSv2 is enabled on all instances launched by Amazon EC2 Auto Scaling groups. The control fails if the Instance Metadata Service (IMDS) version isn't included in the launch configuration or is configured as token optional, which is a setting that allows either IMDSv1 or IMDSv2.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Auto Scaling group launch configurations should configure EC2 instances to require Instance Metadata Service Version 2 (IMDSv2)

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: For information on how to correct this issue, consult the AWS Security Hub controls documentation.

parts.name: overview
parts.prose: This control checks whether an Amazon EC2 Auto Scaling group uses multiple instance types. This control fails if the Amazon EC2 Auto Scaling group has only one instance type defined.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Auto Scaling groups should use multiple instance types in multiple Availability Zones

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: For information on how to correct this issue, consult the AWS Security Hub controls documentation.

parts.name: overview
parts.prose: This control checks whether an Amazon EC2 Auto Scaling group is created from an EC2 launch template. This control fails if an Amazon EC2 Auto Scaling group is not created with a launch template or if a launch template is not specified in a mixed instances policy.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: EC2 Auto Scaling groups should use EC2 launch templates

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: For information on how to correct this issue, consult the AWS Security Hub controls documentation.

parts.name: overview
parts.prose: This control checks whether Amazon EC2 Auto Scaling groups have public IP addresses enabled using launch configurations.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Amazon EC2 instances launched using Auto Scaling group launch configurations should not have Public IP addresses

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: For information on how to correct this issue, consult the AWS Security Hub controls documentation.

parts.name: overview
parts.prose: This control checks if an AWS Backup recovery point is encrypted at rest. The control fails if the recovery point isn't encrypted at rest.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: AWS Backup recovery points should be encrypted at rest

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: For information on how to correct this issue, consult the AWS Security Hub controls documentation.

parts.name: overview
parts.prose: This control checks whether an AWS Batch job queue has tags with the specific keys defined in the parameter requiredKeyTags. The control fails if the job queue doesn't have any tag keys or if it doesn't have all the keys specified in the parameter requiredKeyTags. If the parameter requiredKeyTags isn't provided, the control only checks for the existence of a tag key and fails if the job queue isn't tagged with any key. System tags, which are automatically applied and begin with aws:, are ignored.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Batch job queues should be tagged

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: For information on how to correct this issue, consult the AWS Security Hub controls documentation.

parts.name: overview
parts.prose: This control checks whether an AWS Batch scheduling policy has tags with the specific keys defined in the parameter requiredKeyTags. The control fails if the scheduling policy doesn't have any tag keys or if it doesn't have all the keys specified in the parameter requiredKeyTags. If the parameter requiredKeyTags isn't provided, the control only checks for the existence of a tag key and fails if the scheduling policy isn't tagged with any key. System tags, which are automatically applied and begin with aws:, are ignored.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Batch scheduling policies should be tagged

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: For information on how to correct this issue, consult the AWS Security Hub controls documentation.

parts.name: overview
parts.prose: This control checks whether an AWS Batch compute environment has tags with the specific keys defined in the parameter requiredKeyTags. The control fails if the compute environment doesn't have any tag keys or if it doesn't have all the keys specified in the parameter requiredKeyTags. If the parameter requiredKeyTags isn't provided, the control only checks for the existence of a tag key and fails if the compute environment isn't tagged with any key. System tags, which are automatically applied and begin with aws:, are ignored.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Batch compute environments should be tagged

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: For information on how to correct this issue, consult the AWS Security Hub controls documentation.

parts.name: overview
parts.prose: This control checks whether the compute resources property in a managed AWS Batch compute environment has the tag keys specified by the requiredKeyTags parameter. The control fails if the compute resources property doesn't have any tag keys, or it doesn't have all the keys specified by the requiredKeyTags parameter. If you don't specify any values for the requiredKeyTags parameter, the control checks only for the existence of a tag key and fails if a compute resources property doesn't have any tag keys. The control ignores system tags, which are applied automatically and have the aws: prefix. This control doesn't evaluate unmanaged compute environments, or managed environments that use AWS Fargate resources.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Compute resources properties in managed Batch compute environments should be tagged

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: For information on how to correct this issue, consult the AWS Security Hub controls documentation.

parts.name: overview
parts.prose: This control checks whether an AWS CloudFormation stack has termination protection enabled. The control fails if termination protection is not enabled on a CloudFormation Stack.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: CloudFormation stacks should have termination protection enabled

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: For information on how to correct this issue, consult the AWS Security Hub controls documentation.

parts.name: overview
parts.prose: This control checks whether an AWS CloudFormation stack has a service role associated with it. The control fails for a CloudFormation stack if no service role is associated with it.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: CloudFormation stacks should have associated service roles

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: For information on how to correct this issue, consult the AWS Security Hub controls documentation.

parts.name: overview
parts.prose: This control checks whether an Amazon CloudFront distribution with S3 origins is configured to return a specific object that is the default root object. The control fails if the CloudFront distribution uses S3 origins and doesn't have a default root object configured.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: CloudFront distributions should have a default root object configured

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: For information on how to correct this issue, consult the AWS Security Hub controls documentation.

parts.name: overview
parts.prose: This control checks if CloudFront distributions are using deprecated SSL protocols for HTTPS communication between CloudFront edge locations and your custom origins. This control fails for a CloudFront distribution if it has a 'CustomOriginConfig' where ‘OriginSslProtocols’ includes ‘SSLv3’.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: CloudFront distributions should not use deprecated SSL protocols between edge locations and custom origins

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: For information on how to correct this issue, consult the AWS Security Hub controls documentation.

parts.name: overview
parts.prose: This control checks whether Amazon CloudFront distributions are pointing to non-existent S3 origins. The control fails for a CloudFront distribution if the origin is configured to point to a non-existent bucket. This control only applies to CloudFront distributions where an S3 bucket without static website hosting is the S3 origin.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: CloudFront distributions should not point to non-existent S3 origins

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: For information on how to correct this issue, consult the AWS Security Hub controls documentation.

parts.name: overview
parts.prose: This control checks whether an Amazon CloudFront distribution with an Amazon S3 origin has origin access control (OAC) configured. The control fails if OAC isn't configured.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: CloudFront distributions should use origin access control

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: For information on how to correct this issue, consult the AWS Security Hub controls documentation.

parts.name: overview
parts.prose: This control checks whether an Amazon CloudFront distribution is configured to use the recommended TLS security policy. The control fails if the CloudFront distribution is not configured to use the recommended TLS security policy.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: CloudFront distributions should use the recommended TLS security policy

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: For information on how to correct this issue, consult the AWS Security Hub controls documentation.

parts.name: overview
parts.prose: This control checks whether an Amazon CloudFront distribution with an AWS Lambda function URL as an origin has origin access control (OAC) enabled. The control fails if the CloudFront distribution has a Lambda function URL as an origin and OAC isn't enabled.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: CloudFront distributions should use origin access control for Lambda function URL origins

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: For information on how to correct this issue, consult the AWS Security Hub controls documentation.

parts.name: overview
parts.prose: This control checks whether an Amazon CloudFront distribution is configured to use trusted key groups for signed URL or signed cookie authentication. The control fails if the CloudFront distribution uses trusted signers, or if the distribution has no authentication configured.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: CloudFront distributions should use trusted key groups for signed URLs and cookies

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: For information on how to correct this issue, consult the AWS Security Hub controls documentation.

parts.name: overview
parts.prose: This control checks whether an Amazon CloudFront distribution requires viewers to use HTTPS directly, or whether it uses redirection. The control fails if ViewerProtocolPolicy is set to allow-all for defaultCacheBehavior or for cacheBehaviors.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: CloudFront distributions should require encryption in transit

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: For information on how to correct this issue, consult the AWS Security Hub controls documentation.

parts.name: overview
parts.prose: This control checks whether an Amazon CloudFront distribution is configured with an origin group that has 2 or more origins.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: CloudFront distributions should have origin failover configured

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: For information on how to correct this issue, consult the AWS Security Hub controls documentation.

parts.name: overview
parts.prose: This control checks to see if server access logging is enabled on Amazon CloudFront Distributions. The control will fail if access logging is not enabled for the distribution.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: CloudFront distributions should have logging enabled

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: For information on how to correct this issue, consult the AWS Security Hub controls documentation.

parts.name: overview
parts.prose: This control checks to see if Amazon CloudFront distributions are associated with either WAF or WAFv2 web ACLs. The control fails if a CloudFront distribution is not associated with a web ACL.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: CloudFront distributions should have WAF enabled

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: For information on how to correct this issue, consult the AWS Security Hub controls documentation.

parts.name: overview
parts.prose: This control checks if CloudFront distributions are using the default SSL/TLS certificate CloudFront provides instead of a custom one. This control fails for a CloudFront distribution if it uses the default SSL/TLS certificate.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: CloudFront distributions should use custom SSL/TLS certificates

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: For information on how to correct this issue, consult the AWS Security Hub controls documentation.

parts.name: overview
parts.prose: This control checks if Amazon CloudFront distributions are using a custom SSL/TLS certificate and are configured to use SNI to serve HTTPS requests as opposed to dedicated IP address.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: CloudFront distributions should use SNI to serve HTTPS requests

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: For information on how to correct this issue, consult the AWS Security Hub controls documentation.

parts.name: overview
parts.prose: This control checks if CloudFront distributions are encrypting traffic to custom origins. This control fails if ‘OriginProtocolPolicy’ is ‘http-only’ or if ‘OriginProtocolPolicy’ is ‘match-viewer’ and ‘ViewerProtocolPolicy’ is ‘allow-all’.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: CloudFront distributions should encrypt traffic to custom origins

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: For information on how to correct this issue, consult the AWS Security Hub controls documentation.

parts.name: overview
parts.prose: This AWS control checks that there is at least one multi-region AWS CloudTrail trail includes read and write management events.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: CloudTrail should be enabled and configured with at least one multi-Region trail that includes read and write management events

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: For information on how to correct this issue, consult the AWS Security Hub controls documentation.

parts.name: overview
parts.prose: This control checks whether an AWS CloudTrail Lake event data store is encrypted at rest with a customer managed AWS KMS key. The control fails if the event data store isn't encrypted with a customer managed KMS key. You can optionally specify a list of KMS keys for the control to include in the evaluation.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: CloudTrail Lake event data stores should be encrypted with customer managed AWS KMS keys

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: For information on how to correct this issue, consult the AWS Security Hub controls documentation.

parts.name: overview
parts.prose: This AWS control checks whether AWS CloudTrail is configured to use the server side encryption (SSE) AWS Key Management Service (AWS KMS) customer master key (CMK) encryption. The check will pass if the KmsKeyId is defined.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: CloudTrail should have encryption at-rest enabled

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: For information on how to correct this issue, consult the AWS Security Hub controls documentation.

parts.name: overview
parts.prose: This control checks whether an AWS CloudTrail trail is enabled in your AWS account. The control fails if your account doesn't have at least one CloudTrail trail enabled.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: At least one CloudTrail trail should be enabled

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: For information on how to correct this issue, consult the AWS Security Hub controls documentation.

parts.name: overview
parts.prose: This AWS control checks whether CloudTrail log file validation is enabled.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: CloudTrail log file validation should be enabled

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: For information on how to correct this issue, consult the AWS Security Hub controls documentation.

parts.name: overview
parts.prose: This AWS control checks whether AWS CloudTrail trails are configured to send logs to Amazon CloudWatch Logs.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: CloudTrail trails should be integrated with Amazon CloudWatch Logs

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: For information on how to correct this issue, consult the AWS Security Hub controls documentation.

parts.name: overview
parts.prose: This control checks whether an Amazon CloudWatch alarm has at least one action configured for the 'ALARM' state. The control fails if the alarm doesn't have an action configured for the 'ALARM' state. Optionally, you can include custom parameter values to also require alarm actions for the 'INSUFFICIENT_DATA' or 'OK' states.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: CloudWatch alarms should have specified actions configured

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: For information on how to correct this issue, consult the AWS Security Hub controls documentation.

parts.name: overview
parts.prose: This control checks whether an Amazon CloudWatch log group has a retention period of at least the specified number of days. The control fails if the retention period is less than the specified number. Unless you provide a custom parameter value for the retention period, Security Hub uses a default value of 365 days.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: CloudWatch log groups should be retained for a specified time period

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: For information on how to correct this issue, consult the AWS Security Hub controls documentation.

parts.name: overview
parts.prose: This control evaluates if CloudWatch alarm actions are enabled. The control fails if the alarm action is in the disabled state.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: CloudWatch alarm actions should be enabled

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: For information on how to correct this issue, consult the AWS Security Hub controls documentation.

parts.name: overview
parts.prose: This control checks whether an AWS CodeBuild project Bitbucket source repository URL contains personal access tokens or a user name and password. The control fails if the Bitbucket source repository URL contains personal access tokens or a user name and password.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: CodeBuild Bitbucket source repository URLs should not contain sensitive credentials

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: For information on how to correct this issue, consult the AWS Security Hub controls documentation.

parts.name: overview
parts.prose: This AWS control checks whether the project contains environment variables AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID and AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: CodeBuild project environment variables should not contain clear text credentials

parts.name: guidance

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: For information on how to correct this issue, consult the AWS Security Hub controls documentation.

parts.name: overview
parts.prose: This control checks if EC2 Transit Gateways are automatically accepting shared VPC attachments requests. This control will fail for a Transit Gateway that automatically accept shared VPC attachment requests.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: EC2 Transit Gateways should not automatically accept VPC attachment requests

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: For information on how to correct this issue, consult the AWS Security Hub controls documentation.

parts.name: overview
parts.prose: This control checks whether the virtualization type of an EC2 instance is paravirtual. The control fails for an EC2 instance if ‘virtualizationType’ is set to ‘paravirtual’.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: EC2 paravirtual instance types should not be used

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: For information on how to correct this issue, consult the AWS Security Hub controls documentation.

parts.name: overview
parts.prose: This control checks if Amazon EC2 launch templates are configured to assign public IP addresses to network interfaces upon launch. The control fails if an EC2 launch template is configured to assign a public IP address to network interfaces or if there is at least one network interface that has a public IP address.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: EC2 launch templates should not assign public IPs to network interfaces

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: For information on how to correct this issue, consult the AWS Security Hub controls documentation.

parts.name: overview
parts.prose: This control evaluates if an Amazon EBS volume in 'in-use' state is covered by a backup plan. The control fails if an EBS volume isn’t covered by a backup plan. If you set the 'backupVaultLockCheck' parameter equal to 'true', the control passes only if the EBS volume is backed up in an AWS Backup locked vault.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: EBS volumes should be in a backup plan

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: For information on how to correct this issue, consult the AWS Security Hub controls documentation.

parts.name: overview
parts.prose: This AWS control checks whether the EBS volumes that are in an attached state are encrypted.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Attached EBS volumes should be encrypted at-rest

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: For information on how to correct this issue, consult the AWS Security Hub controls documentation.

parts.name: overview
parts.prose: This control checks whether an Amazon EC2 instance has been stopped for longer than the allowed number of days. The control fails if an EC2 instance is stopped for longer than the maximum allowed time period. Unless you provide a custom parameter value for the maximum allowed time period, Security Hub uses a default value of 30 days.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Stopped EC2 instances should be removed after a specified time period

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: For information on how to correct this issue, consult the AWS Security Hub controls documentation.

parts.name: overview
parts.prose: This control checks if an AWS Client VPN endpoint has client connection logging enabled. The control fails if the endpoint doesn't have client connection logging enabled.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: EC2 Client VPN endpoints should have client connection logging enabled

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: For information on how to correct this issue, consult the AWS Security Hub controls documentation.

parts.name: overview
parts.prose: This control checks whether an Amazon EC2 security group allows ingress from 0.0.0.0/0 to remote server administration ports (ports 22 and 3389). The control fails if the security group allows ingress from 0.0.0.0/0 to port 22 or 3389.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: EC2 security groups should not allow ingress from 0.0.0.0/0 to remote server administration ports

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: For information on how to correct this issue, consult the AWS Security Hub controls documentation.

parts.name: overview
parts.prose: This control checks whether an Amazon EC2 security group allows ingress traffic from ::/0 to remote server administration ports (ports 22 and 3389). The control fails if the security group allows ingress traffic from ::/0 to port 22 or 3389.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: EC2 security groups should not allow ingress from ::/0 to remote server administration ports

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: For information on how to correct this issue, consult the AWS Security Hub controls documentation.

parts.name: overview
parts.prose: This control checks whether a virtual private cloud (VPC) that you manage has an interface VPC endpoint for ECR API. The control fails if the VPC doesn't have an interface VPC endpoint for ECR API. This control evaluates resources in a single account.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: VPCs should be configured with an interface endpoint for ECR API

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: For information on how to correct this issue, consult the AWS Security Hub controls documentation.

parts.name: overview
parts.prose: This control checks whether a virtual private cloud (VPC) that you manage has an interface VPC endpoint for Docker Registry. The control fails if the VPC doesn't have an interface VPC endpoint for Docker Registry. This control evaluates resources in a single account.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: VPCs should be configured with an interface endpoint for Docker Registry

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: For information on how to correct this issue, consult the AWS Security Hub controls documentation.

parts.name: overview
parts.prose: This control checks whether a virtual private cloud (VPC) that you manage has an interface VPC endpoint for Systems Manager. The control fails if the VPC doesn't have an interface VPC endpoint for Systems Manager. This control evaluates resources in a single account.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: VPCs should be configured with an interface endpoint for Systems Manager

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: For information on how to correct this issue, consult the AWS Security Hub controls documentation.

parts.name: overview
parts.prose: This control checks whether a virtual private cloud (VPC) that you manage has an interface VPC endpoint for Systems Manager Incident Manager Contacts. The control fails if the VPC doesn't have an interface VPC endpoint for Systems Manager Incident Manager Contacts. This control evaluates resources in a single account.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: VPCs should be configured with an interface endpoint for Systems Manager Incident Manager Contacts

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: For information on how to correct this issue, consult the AWS Security Hub controls documentation.

parts.name: overview
parts.prose: This control checks whether Amazon Virtual Private Cloud flow logs are found and enabled for Amazon VPCs. The traffic type is set to 'Reject'.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: VPC flow logging should be enabled in all VPCs

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: For information on how to correct this issue, consult the AWS Security Hub controls documentation.

parts.name: overview
parts.prose: This control checks whether a virtual private cloud (VPC) that you manage has an interface VPC endpoint for Systems Manager Incident Manager. The control fails if the VPC doesn't have an interface VPC endpoint for Systems Manager Incident Manager. This control evaluates resources in a single account.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: VPCs should be configured with an interface endpoint for Systems Manager Incident Manager

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: For information on how to correct this issue, consult the AWS Security Hub controls documentation.

parts.name: overview
parts.prose: This control checks whether Amazon Elastic Block Store (EBS) encryption is enabled by default. The control fails if EBS default encryption is not enabled.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: EBS default encryption should be enabled

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: For information on how to correct this issue, consult the AWS Security Hub controls documentation.

parts.name: overview
parts.prose: This control checks whether your Amazon Elastic Compute Cloud (Amazon EC2) instance metadata version is configured with Instance Metadata Service Version 2 (IMDSv2). The control passes if HttpTokens is set to required for IMDSv2. The control fails if HttpTokens is set to optional.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: EC2 instances should use Instance Metadata Service Version 2 (IMDSv2)

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: For information on how to correct this issue, consult the AWS Security Hub controls documentation.

parts.name: overview
parts.prose: This control checks whether EC2 instances have a public IP address. The control fails if the publicIp field is present in the EC2 instance configuration item. This control applies to IPv4 addresses only.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: EC2 instances should not have a public IPv4 address

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: For information on how to correct this issue, consult the AWS Security Hub controls documentation.

parts.name: overview
parts.prose: This control checks whether a private ECR repository has image scanning configured. This control fails if a private ECR repository doesn’t have image scanning configured.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: ECR private repositories should have image scanning configured

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: For information on how to correct this issue, consult the AWS Security Hub controls documentation.

parts.name: overview
parts.prose: This control checks whether a private ECR repository has tag immutability configured. This control fails if a private ECR repository has tag immutability disabled. This rule passes if tag immutability is configured and has the value IMMUTABLE

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: ECR private repositories should have tag immutability configured

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: For information on how to correct this issue, consult the AWS Security Hub controls documentation.

parts.name: overview
parts.prose: This control checks whether an ECR repository has at least one lifecycle policy configured. This control fails if an ECR repository does not have any lifecycle policies configured.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: ECR repositories should have at least one lifecycle policy configured

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: For information on how to correct this issue, consult the AWS Security Hub controls documentation.

parts.name: overview
parts.prose: This control checks whether an Amazon ECR repository is encrypted at rest with a customer managed AWS KMS key. The control fails if the ECR repository isn't encrypted with a customer managed KMS key. You can optionally specify a list of KMS keys for the control to include in the evaluation.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: ECR repositories should be encrypted with customer managed AWS KMS keys

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: For information on how to correct this issue, consult the AWS Security Hub controls documentation.

parts.name: overview
parts.prose: This control checks if an Amazon ECS Task Definition with host networking mode has "privileged" or "user" container definitions. The control fails with host network mode and container definitions are privileged=false or empty and user=root or empty.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Amazon ECS task definitions should have secure networking modes and user definitions.

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: For information on how to correct this issue, consult the AWS Security Hub controls documentation.

parts.name: overview
parts.prose: This control checks if ECS Fargate services is running the latest Fargate platform version. This control fails if the platform version is not latest.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: ECS Fargate services should run on the latest Fargate platform version

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: For information on how to correct this issue, consult the AWS Security Hub controls documentation.

parts.name: overview
parts.prose: This control checks if ECS clusters use Container Insights. This control fails if Container Insights are not set up for a cluster.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: ECS clusters should use Container Insights

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: For information on how to correct this issue, consult the AWS Security Hub controls documentation.

parts.name: overview
parts.prose: This control checks whether the latest active revision of an Amazon ECS task definition uses host network mode. The control fails if the latest active revision of the ECS task definition uses host network mode.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: ECS task definitions should not use host network mode

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: For information on how to correct this issue, consult the AWS Security Hub controls documentation.

parts.name: overview
parts.prose: This control checks whether the latest active revision of an Amazon ECS Task Definition uses in-transit encryption for EFS volumes. The control fails if the latest active revision of the ECS Task Definition has in-transit encryption disabled for EFS volumes.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: ECS Task Definitions should use in-transit encryption for EFS volumes

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: For information on how to correct this issue, consult the AWS Security Hub controls documentation.

parts.name: overview
parts.prose: The control checks whether an Amazon ECS capacity provider has managed termination protection enabled. The control fails if managed termination protection is not enabled on an ECS capacity provider.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: ECS capacity providers should have managed termination protection enabled

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: For information on how to correct this issue, consult the AWS Security Hub controls documentation.

parts.name: overview
parts.prose: This control checks whether the latest active revision of an Amazon ECS task definition configures Linux containers to run as non-root users.The control fails if a default root user is configured or user configuration is absent for any container.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: ECS task definitions should configure non-root users in Linux container definitions

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: For information on how to correct this issue, consult the AWS Security Hub controls documentation.

parts.name: overview
parts.prose: This control checks whether the latest active revision of an Amazon ECS task definition configures Windows containers to run as users that are not default administrators.The control fails if a default administrator is configured as user or user configuration is absent for any container.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: ECS task definitions should configure non-administrator users in Windows container definitions

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: For information on how to correct this issue, consult the AWS Security Hub controls documentation.

parts.name: overview
parts.prose: This control checks if Amazon ECS task definitions are configured to share a host's process namespace with its containers. This control fails if the task definition shares the host's process namespace with the containers running on it

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: ECS task definitions should not share the host's process namespace

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: For information on how to correct this issue, consult the AWS Security Hub controls documentation.

parts.name: overview
parts.prose: This control checks if the privileged parameter in the container definition of Amazon ECS task definitions is set to 'true'. This control fails if this parameter is equal to 'true'.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: ECS containers should run as non-privileged

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: For information on how to correct this issue, consult the AWS Security Hub controls documentation.

parts.name: overview
parts.prose: This control checks if ECS task definitions configure containers to be limited to read-only access to mounted root filesystems. This control fails if the ReadonlyRootFilesystem parameter in the container definition of ECS task definitions is set to ‘false’.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: ECS task definitions should configure containers to be limited to read-only access to root filesystems

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: For information on how to correct this issue, consult the AWS Security Hub controls documentation.

parts.name: overview
parts.prose: This control checks if the key value of any variables in the ‘environment’ parameter of container definitions includes - AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID, AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY,  or ECS_ENGINE_AUTH_DATA.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Secrets should not be passed as container environment variables

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: For information on how to correct this issue, consult the AWS Security Hub controls documentation.

parts.name: overview
parts.prose: This control checks if the latest active Amazon ECS task definition has a logging configuration specified. The control fails if the task definition doesn't have the logConfiguration property defined or if the value for logDriver is null in at least one container definition.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: ECS task definitions should have a logging configuration

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: For information on how to correct this issue, consult the AWS Security Hub controls documentation.

parts.name: overview
parts.prose: This AWS control checks whether Amazon Elastic File System (Amazon EFS) is configured to encrypt the file data using AWS Key Management Service (AWS KMS). The check will fail if the encrypted key is set to false on DescribeFileSystems or if the KmsKeyId key on DescribeFileSystems does not match the KmsKeyId parameter.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Elastic File System should be configured to encrypt file data at-rest using AWS KMS

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: For information on how to correct this issue, consult the AWS Security Hub controls documentation.

parts.name: overview
parts.prose: This control checks whether Amazon Elastic File System (Amazon EFS) file systems are added to the backup plans in AWS Backup. The control fails if EFS file systems are not included in the backup plans.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Amazon EFS volumes should be in backup plans

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: For information on how to correct this issue, consult the AWS Security Hub controls documentation.

parts.name: overview
parts.prose: This control checks if Amazon Elastic File System (Amazon EFS) access points are configured to enforce a root directory. This control fails if the value of 'Path' is set to '/' (default root directory of the file system).

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: EFS access points should enforce a root directory

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: For information on how to correct this issue, consult the AWS Security Hub controls documentation.

parts.name: overview
parts.prose: This control checks whether Amazon Elastic File System (Amazon EFS) access points are configured to enforce a user identity. This control fails if ‘PosixUser’ is not defined under ‘configuration’ or if parameters are provided and there is no match in the corresponding parameter.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: EFS access points should enforce a user identity

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: For information on how to correct this issue, consult the AWS Security Hub controls documentation.

parts.name: overview
parts.prose: This control checks whether an Amazon EFS mount target is associated with subnets that assign public IP addresses on launch. The control fails if the mount target is associated with subnets that assign public IP addresses on launch.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: EFS mount targets should not be associated with subnets that assign public IP addresses on launch

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: For information on how to correct this issue, consult the AWS Security Hub controls documentation.

parts.name: overview
parts.prose: This control checks whether an Amazon EFS file system has automatic backups enabled. This control fails if the EFS file system doesn't have automatic backups enabled.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: EFS file systems should have automatic backups enabled

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: For information on how to correct this issue, consult the AWS Security Hub controls documentation.

parts.name: overview
parts.prose: This control checks whether an Amazon EFS file system encrypts data with AWS Key Management Service (AWS KMS). The control fails if a file system isn't encrypted. Optionally, you can include the kmsKeyArns parameter to check if a file system is encrypted with specified KMS keys.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: EFS file systems should be encrypted at rest

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: For information on how to correct this issue, consult the AWS Security Hub controls documentation.

parts.name: overview
parts.prose: This control checks whether an Amazon EKS cluster endpoint is publicly accessible. The control fails if an EKS cluster has an endpoint that is publicly accessible.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: EKS cluster endpoints should not be publicly accessible

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: For information on how to correct this issue, consult the AWS Security Hub controls documentation.

parts.name: overview
parts.prose: This control checks whether an EKS cluster is running on a supported Kubernetes version. The control fails if the EKS cluster is running on an unsupported version.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: EKS clusters should run on a supported Kubernetes version

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: For information on how to correct this issue, consult the AWS Security Hub controls documentation.

parts.name: overview
parts.prose: This control checks whether an Amazon EKS cluster uses encrypted Kubernetes secrets. The control fails if the cluster's Kubernetes secrets aren't encrypted.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: EKS clusters should use encrypted Kubernetes secrets

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: For information on how to correct this issue, consult the AWS Security Hub controls documentation.

parts.name: overview
parts.prose: This control checks whether an Amazon EKS cluster has audit logging enabled. The control fails if audit logging isn't enabled for the cluster.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: EKS clusters should have audit logging enabled

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: For information on how to correct this issue, consult the AWS Security Hub controls documentation.

parts.name: overview
parts.prose: This AWS control checks whether HTTP to HTTPS redirection is configured on all HTTP listeners of Application Load Balancers. The control will fail if one or more HTTP listeners of Application Load Balancers do not have HTTP to HTTPS redirection configured.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Application Load Balancer should be configured to redirect all HTTP requests to HTTPS

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: For information on how to correct this issue, consult the AWS Security Hub controls documentation.

parts.name: overview
parts.prose: This control checks whether a Classic Load Balancer has been configured to span at least the specified number of Availability Zones (AZs). The control fails if the Classic Load Balancer does not span at least the specified number of AZs. Unless you provide a custom parameter value for the minimum number of AZs, Security Hub uses a default value of two AZs.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Classic Load Balancer should span multiple Availability Zones

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: For information on how to correct this issue, consult the AWS Security Hub controls documentation.

parts.name: overview
parts.prose: This control checks whether the Application Load Balancer is configured with defensive or strictest de-sync mitigation mode. This control fails if the Application Load Balancer is not configured with defensive or strictest desync mitigation mode.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Application Load Balancer should be configured with defensive or strictest desync mitigation mode

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: For information on how to correct this issue, consult the AWS Security Hub controls documentation.

parts.name: overview
parts.prose: This control checks whether an Elastic Load Balancer V2 (Application, Network, or Gateway Load Balancer) has registered instances from at least the specified number of Availability Zones (AZs). The control fails if an Elastic Load Balancer V2 doesn't have instances registered in at least the specified number of AZs. Unless you provide a custom parameter value for the minimum number of AZs, Security Hub uses a default value of two AZs.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Application, Network and Gateway Load Balancers should span multiple Availability Zones

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: For information on how to correct this issue, consult the AWS Security Hub controls documentation.

parts.name: overview
parts.prose: This control checks whether the Classic Load Balancer is configured with defensive or strictest desync mitigation mode. This control will fail if the Classic Load Balancer is not configured with defensive or strictest desync mitigation mode.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Classic Load Balancer should be configured with defensive or strictest desync mitigation mode

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: For information on how to correct this issue, consult the AWS Security Hub controls documentation.

parts.name: overview
parts.prose: This control checks whether Application Load Balancers are associated with an AWS WAF web ACL. The control fails if the Enabled field for the WAF configuration is set to false.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Application Load Balancers should be associated with an AWS WAF web ACL

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: For information on how to correct this issue, consult the AWS Security Hub controls documentation.

parts.name: overview
parts.prose: This control checks whether the HTTPS listener for an Application Load Balancer or the TLS listener for a Network Load Balancer is configured to encrypt data in transit by using a recommended security policy. The control fails if the HTTPS or TLS listener for a load balancer isn't configured to use a recommended security policy.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Application and Network Load Balancers with listeners should use recommended security policies

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: For information on how to correct this issue, consult the AWS Security Hub controls documentation.

parts.name: overview
parts.prose: This control checks whether the listener for an Application Load Balancer or Network Load Balancer is configured to use a secure protocol for encryption of data in transit. The control fails if an Application Load Balancer listener isn't configured to use the HTTPS protocol, or a Network Load Balancer listener isn't configured to use the TLS protocol.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Application and Network Load Balancer listeners should use secure protocols to encrypt data in transit

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: For information on how to correct this issue, consult the AWS Security Hub controls documentation.

parts.name: overview
parts.prose: This control checks whether a Classic Load Balancer uses HTTPS/SSL certificates provided by AWS Certificate Manager. The control fails if a Classic Load Balancer that is configured with an HTTPS/SSL listener does not use a certificate provided by AWS Certificate Manager.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Classic Load Balancers with SSL/HTTPS listeners should use a certificate provided by AWS Certificate Manager

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: For information on how to correct this issue, consult the AWS Security Hub controls documentation.

parts.name: overview
parts.prose: This control checks whether the target group for application and network load balancer health checks use an encrypted transport protocol. The control fails if the health check protocol does not use HTTPS. This control is not applicable to Lambda target types.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Application and Network Load Balancer target groups should use encrypted health check protocols

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: For information on how to correct this issue, consult the AWS Security Hub controls documentation.

parts.name: overview
parts.prose: This control checks whether an Elastic Load Balancing target group uses an encrypted transport protocol. This control does not apply to target groups with a target type of Lambda or ALB, or target groups using the GENEVE protocol. The control fails if the target group does not use HTTPS, TLS, or QUIC protocol.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: ELB target groups should use encrypted transport protocols

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: For information on how to correct this issue, consult the AWS Security Hub controls documentation.

parts.name: overview
parts.prose: This control checks whether your Classic Load Balancer listeners are configured with HTTPS or TLS protocol for front-end (client to load balancer) connections. The control is applicable if a Classic Load Balancer has listeners. If your Classic Load Balancer does not have a listener configured, then the control does not report any findings.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Classic Load Balancer listeners should be configured with HTTPS or TLS termination

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: For information on how to correct this issue, consult the AWS Security Hub controls documentation.

parts.name: overview
parts.prose: This control evaluates AWS Application Load Balancers (ALB) to ensure they are configured to drop invalid http headers. By default, ALBs are not configured to drop invalid http header values. This control evaluates all ALBs fails if the attribute value of routing.http.drop_invalid_header_fields.enabled is set to false.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Application load balancer should be configured to drop invalid http headers

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: For information on how to correct this issue, consult the AWS Security Hub controls documentation.

parts.name: overview
parts.prose: This control checks whether the Application Load Balancer and the Classic Load Balancer have logging enabled. The control fails if the access_logs.s3.enabled is false.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Application and Classic Load Balancers logging should be enabled

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: For information on how to correct this issue, consult the AWS Security Hub controls documentation.

parts.name: overview
parts.prose: This control checks whether Application, Gateway, and Network Load Balancers have deletion protection enabled. The control fails if deletion protection is disabled.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Application, Gateway, and Network Load Balancers should have deletion protection enabled

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: For information on how to correct this issue, consult the AWS Security Hub controls documentation.

parts.name: overview
parts.prose: This control checks whether your Classic Load Balancer SSL listeners use the predefined policy ELBSecurityPolicy-TLS-1-2-2017-01. The control fails if the Classic Load Balancer SSL listeners do not use the predefined policy ELBSecurityPolicy-TLS-1-2-2017-01.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Classic Load Balancers with SSL listeners should use a predefined security policy that has strong configuration

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: For information on how to correct this issue, consult the AWS Security Hub controls documentation.

parts.name: overview
parts.prose: This control checks whether cross-zone load balancing is enabled for Classic Load Balancers. This control fails if cross-zone load balancing is not enabled for a Classic Load Balancer.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Classic Load Balancers should have cross-zone load balancing enabled

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: For information on how to correct this issue, consult the AWS Security Hub controls documentation.

parts.name: overview
parts.prose: This control checks whether primary nodes on EMR clusters have public IP addresses.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Amazon EMR cluster primary nodes should not have public IP addresses

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: For information on how to correct this issue, consult the AWS Security Hub controls documentation.

parts.name: overview
parts.prose: This control checks whether your account is configured with Amazon EMR block public access. The control fails if the block public access setting is not enabled or if any port other than 22 is allowed.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Amazon EMR block public access setting should be enabled

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: For information on how to correct this issue, consult the AWS Security Hub controls documentation.

parts.name: overview
parts.prose: This control checks whether an Amazon EMR security configuration has encryption at rest enabled. The control fails if the security configuration doesn't enable encryption at rest.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Amazon EMR security configurations should have encryption at rest enabled

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: For information on how to correct this issue, consult the AWS Security Hub controls documentation.

parts.name: overview
parts.prose: This control checks whether an Amazon EMR security configuration has encryption in transit enabled. The control fails if the security configuration doesn't enable encryption in transit.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Amazon EMR security configurations should have encryption in transit enabled

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: For information on how to correct this issue, consult the AWS Security Hub controls documentation.

parts.name: overview
parts.prose: This AWS control checks whether Elasticsearch domains have encryption at rest configuration enabled. This check fails if the EncryptionAtRestOptions field is not enabled.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Elasticsearch domains should have encryption at-rest enabled

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: For information on how to correct this issue, consult the AWS Security Hub controls documentation.

parts.name: overview
parts.prose: This control checks whether an Elasticsearch domain is publicly accessible. The control fails if an Elasticsearch domain is publicly accessible. It does not evaluate the VPC subnet routing configuration to determine public access.

parts.name: statement

parts.name: overview
parts.prose:
This AWS control checks whether the account password policy for IAM users uses the following minimum PCI DSS configurations:

RequireUppercaseCharacters: Require at least one uppercase character in password. (Default = true)
RequireLowercaseCharacters: Require at least one lowercase character in password. (Default = true)
RequireNumbers: Require at least one number in password. (Default = true)
MinimumPasswordLength: Password minimum length. (Default = 7 or longer)
PasswordReusePrevention: Number of passwords before allowing reuse. (Default = 4)
MaxPasswordAge: Number of days before password expiration. (Default = 90)

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Password policies for IAM users should have strong configurations

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: For information on how to correct this issue, consult the AWS Security Hub controls documentation.

parts.name: overview
parts.prose: Password policies are, in part, used to enforce password complexity requirements. IAM password policies can be used to ensure passwords are comprised of different character sets. It is recommended that the password policy require at least one uppercase letter.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Ensure IAM password policy requires at least one uppercase letter

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: For information on how to correct this issue, consult the AWS Security Hub controls documentation.

parts.prose: This control checks whether Amazon Inspector Lambda standard scanning is enabled. parts.prose: This control checks whether an AWS IoT Events input has tags with the specific keys defined in the parameter requiredKeyTags. The control fails if the input doesn't have any tag keys or if it doesn't have all the keys specified in the parameter requiredKeyTags. If the parameter requiredKeyTags isn't provided, the control only checks for the existence of a tag key and fails if the input isn't tagged with any key. System tags, which are automatically applied and begin with aws:, are ignored.

parts.prose: Checks whether the default version of IAM customer managed policies allow principals to use the AWS Key Management Service (KMS) decryption actions on all resources. This control fails if kms:Decrypt or kms:ReEncryptFrom actions are allowed on all KMS keys. The control evaluates both attached and unattached customer managed policies. It does not check inline policies or AWS managed policies.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: IAM customer managed policies should not allow decryption actions on all KMS keys

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: For information on how to correct this issue, consult the AWS Security Hub controls documentation.

parts.name: overview
parts.prose: Checks whether the inline policies embedded in your IAM principals (Role/User/Group) allow the AWS Key Management Service (KMS) decryption actions on all KMS keys. This control fails if kms:Decrypt or kms:ReEncryptFrom actions are allowed on all KMS keys in an inline policy.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: IAM principals should not have IAM inline policies that allow decryption actions on all KMS keys

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: For information on how to correct this issue, consult the AWS Security Hub controls documentation.

parts.name: overview
parts.prose: This AWS control checks that key rotation is enabled for each AWS KMS key. It does not check KMS keys that have imported key material.

parts.name: statement

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: For information on how to correct this issue, consult the AWS Security Hub controls documentation.

parts.name: overview

The control fails if the data retention period is less than the specified time frame. Unless you provide a custom parameter value for the data retention period, Security Hub uses a default value of 168 hours.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Kinesis streams should have an adequate data retention period

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: For information on how to correct this issue, consult the AWS Security Hub controls documentation.

parts.name: overview
parts.prose: This control checks whether the AWS Lambda function policy attached to the Lambda resource prohibits public access. If the Lambda function policy allows public access, the control fails.

parts.name: statement

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: For information on how to correct this issue, consult the AWS Security Hub controls documentation.

parts.name: overview
parts.prose: This control checks whether Lambda function runtime settings match the expected values set for the supported runtimes in each language. The control fails if a Lambda function doesn't use a supported runtime.

parts.name: statement

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: For information on how to correct this issue, consult the AWS Security Hub controls documentation.

parts.name: guidance

parts.name: overview
parts.prose: This control checks if an AWS Lambda function that connects to a virtual private cloud (VPC) operates in at least the specified number of Availability Zone (AZs). The control fails if the function doesn't operate in at least the specified number of AZs. Unless you provide a custom parameter value for the minimum number of AZs, Security Hub uses a default value of two AZs.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: VPC Lambda functions should operate in multiple Availability Zones

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: For information on how to correct this issue, consult the AWS Security Hub controls documentation.

parts.name: overview
parts.prose: This control checks whether active tracing with AWS X-Ray is enabled for an AWS Lambda function. The control fails if active tracing with X-Ray is disabled for the Lambda function.

parts.name: statement

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: For information on how to correct this issue, consult the AWS Security Hub controls documentation.

parts.prose: For information on how to correct this issue, consult the AWS Security Hub controls documentation.

parts.name: overview
parts.prose: This control checks whether the deployment mode for an Amazon MQ ActiveMQ broker is set to active/standby. The control fails if a single-instance broker (enabled by default) is set as the deployment mode.

parts.name: overview
parts.prose: This controls checks if a MSK cluster allows encryption in transit using HTTPS(TLS) among the broker nodes of the cluster. The control fails if plain text communication is enabled for in cluster broker node connection.

parts.name: statement

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: For information on how to correct this issue, consult the AWS Security Hub controls documentation.

parts.name: overview
parts.prose: This control checks whether an Amazon MSK cluster has enhanced monitoring configured, specified by a monitoring level of at least "PER_TOPIC_PER_BROKER". The control fails if the monitoring level for the cluster is set to "DEFAULT" or "PER_BROKER".

parts.name: statement

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: For information on how to correct this issue, consult the AWS Security Hub controls documentation.

parts.name: overview
parts.prose: This control checks whether public access is disabled for an Amazon MSK cluster. The control fails if public access is enabled for the MSK cluster.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Amazon MSK clusters should have public access disabled

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: For information on how to correct this issue, consult the AWS Security Hub controls documentation.

parts.name: overview
parts.prose: This control checks whether logging is enabled for an Amazon MSK connector. The control fails if logging is disabled for the MSK connector.

parts.name: statement

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: For information on how to correct this issue, consult the AWS Security Hub controls documentation.

parts.name: overview
parts.prose: This control checks whether unauthenticated access is enabled for an Amazon MSK cluster. The control fails if unauthenticated access is enabled for the MSK cluster.

parts.name: statement

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: For information on how to correct this issue, consult the AWS Security Hub controls documentation.

parts.name: overview

parts.name: statement

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: For information on how to correct this issue, consult the AWS Security Hub controls documentation.

parts.prose: This control checks whether a Neptune DB cluster is encrypted at rest. The control fails if a Neptune DB cluster isn't encrypted at rest.

parts.name: statement
